explanation_md: `Tag<int>` and `Tag<double>` have no pointer, so the primary template fires: `generic`. `Tag<int*>` and `Tag<double*>` both match the partial specialization `Tag<T*>`, printing `pointer`. The partial specialization matches any pointer type, not just `int*`.

prompt_md: Why can't you write a partial specialization of a function template in C++?

options.a: Function templates can only be specialized with `template <>`, not `template <typename T>`.
options.b: The C++ standard simply does not allow partial specializations for function templates — only class templates support them.
options.c: Function templates cannot be specialized at all; you must use virtual functions instead.
options.d: Partial function specialization is allowed but only inside a namespace.

explanation_md: The C++ standard explicitly forbids partial specialization of function templates. The usual workaround is a non-template function overload or a partially specialized helper class that the function delegates to. Full specializations (`template <>`) are allowed for function templates.

options.a: `Printer<T*>` should be written as `Printer<typename T*>` to declare the pointer type.
options.b: The header `template <>` has empty brackets, but partial specializations still have open parameters — it should be `template <typename T>`.
options.c: The `print` function in the specialization should be non-static.
options.d: `*v` in the specialization body dereferences a potentially null pointer.

explanation_md: A partial specialization still has open template parameters (here `T` is still unknown), so the header must be `template <typename T>`. Using `template <>` would mean all parameters are fixed, but `T*` still contains the free parameter `T` — the compiler reports an error that `T` is undeclared.

prompt_md: You have `template <typename T, typename U> class Pair {};` and two partial specializations: `Pair<T, int>` and `Pair<int, T>`. What happens when you instantiate `Pair<int, int>`?

options.a: The compiler picks `Pair<T, int>` because it was declared first.
options.b: The compiler picks `Pair<int, T>` because it is more specific.
options.c: The compiler reports an ambiguity error — both specializations match.
options.d: The compiler falls back to the primary template.

explanation_md: `Pair<int, int>` matches both `Pair<T, int>` (with T=int) and `Pair<int, T>` (with T=int). Neither is more specialized than the other, so the compiler cannot choose and emits an ambiguity error. Specializations must be designed to be non-overlapping.

`Buf<int, 3>` does not match the `N=1` partial specialization, so the primary template prints `size 3`. `Buf<double, 1>` and `Buf<char, 1>` both have `N=1`, matching the partial specialization and printing `single` each.
